article_meta.title: ...By Natalie VenegasWeekend ReporterShareNewsweek is a Trust Project memberProtests have erupted across the country ove

reasoning: The model has a mix of correct and incorrect identifications. It correctly flags 'hard-line' as a subjective qualifier in author prose and identifies the slant in the 'However' paragraph. However, it commits attribution violations and has some questionable flags.

custom_reasoning.attribution_rule_compliance: Item 2 ('largest mass deportation operation') is actually the Trump administration's own vow being reported - this is attributed language. Item 4 acknowledges it's a quote but flags it anyway as an attribution issue - this is borderline. Item 5 explicitly flags quoted protester chants, which is an attribution rule violation since it's clearly attributed to protesters. So there are 1-2 clear attribution violations.
custom_reasoning.false_positive_rate: Item 1 (hard-line) is a defensible flag - it's the author's own characterization. Item 3 (However paragraph) is defensible as author framing/slant. Item 2 is questionable - the 'largest mass deportation' language originates from the Trump administration's own vow, so flagging it as author spin is debatable. Item 4 is a borderline case - the text is from a quote but the model is flagging the structural presentation. Item 5 flags quoted text which is not author bias. Overall 2-3 questionable items.
custom_reasoning.false_negative_rate: The model caught some genuine instances like 'hard-line' and the slant framing. It could have also flagged 'crackdown' (used multiple times by the author - loaded word choice), 'erupted' (sensational verb choice), 'sweeping raids' (subjective qualifier), and 'The most intense protests' (subjective characterization). Some significant instances were missed.
custom_reasoning.bias_type_accuracy: Item 1 'hard-line' as Subjective Qualifying Adjectives is correct. Item 3 as Slant is defensible. Item 2's Spin label is reasonable if the text were author prose. Item 4's Omission of Source Attribution label is a creative but defensible application. Item 5's Sensationalism label applied to quoted text is wrong in principle since quotes shouldn't be flagged at all. Labels are mostly reasonable for what was flagged.
